subsystem: Exiv2 PNG metadata handling: PngChunk::readRawProfile, the routine that decodes an ImageMagick-style "raw profile" text chunk (tEXt / zTXt / iTXt) embedded in a PNG image into a binary DataBuf. It parses an ASCII header (a leading name line, then whitespace, then a decimal length, then a hex-encoded blob) and converts the hex digits back into raw bytes.
provenance: Identified by the fully-qualified method name PngChunk::readRawProfile (line 2), the Exiv2-specific types DataBuf (with .size_/.pData_/.alloc members) and the Exiv2::kerCorruptedMetadata error code and enforce() macro (lines 67-68, 93, 105), and the debug strings "Exiv2::PngChunk::readRawProfile: ..." (lines 74, 81, 99). This is the well-known Exiv2 raw-profile decoder from src/pngchunk_int.cpp. The banner comment /* sample: primevul_train_3478_vuln */ (line 1) and file name eval_0070_cwe770.cpp indicate a PrimeVul sample tagged with CWE-770.
trust_boundary: The single parameter `text` (a DataBuf) is fully attacker-controlled: it is the raw bytes of a PNG text chunk read from an image file (for zTXt it is the zlib-inflated payload). Both text.pData_ (the byte buffer) and text.size_ (its length) come straight off the wire/file with no prior validation. Every pointer walk (sp/eot), the ASCII decimal length parsed by atol, and every hex byte fed into the unhex[] table are therefore hostile inputs. iTXt (the bool) is also caller/format-derived. Nothing in this function is trusted.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The function signature. It is a member of PngChunk and returns a DataBuf (Exiv2's owning byte-buffer type with fields size_ and pData_). `text` is a const reference to the source buffer — the untrusted PNG text-chunk payload. `iTXt` selects between two decoding paths: when true the payload is treated as an already-binary iTXt profile and copied verbatim; when false the payload is an ImageMagick raw-profile ASCII blob that must be hex-decoded. The returned DataBuf owns freshly allocated memory holding the decoded profile.

`info` is the output buffer that will be returned. `unhex[103]` is a stack lookup table mapping an ASCII byte value directly to its hex nibble value, used later as unhex[*sp]. Working out the indices: index 48 ('0') through 57 ('9') map to 0..9 (the trailing '1' at index 49 and the '2,3,4,5,6,7,8,9' at indices 50-57), and indices 97 ('a') through 102 ('f') map to 10..15 (the '10,11,12' ending the fifth row lands on 97-99 and '13,14,15' on 100-102). Every other index is 0. The array size is 103, so the only valid indices are 0..102, and 'f' (102) is exactly the last element — the table is sized with zero slack above 'f'.

Early-out for an empty payload: if there are zero bytes, return an empty DataBuf. This guarantees text.size_ >= 1 for the remainder of the function, which is what makes the later pData_+1 indexing not immediately point past a zero-length buffer.

The iTXt fast path. It allocates an output buffer of exactly text.size_ bytes and memcpy's the whole input into it, returning a verbatim copy. There is no parsing here — the size of the allocation and the copy length are both driven directly by the attacker-controlled text.size_. The memcpy is internally consistent (dest was just alloc'd to size_ and src has size_ bytes), so it is not itself an overflow; the security-relevant property is that the allocation magnitude is chosen by the attacker.

Sets up a cursor pair for the raw-profile parse. `sp` (the running read cursor) starts at pData_+1, deliberately skipping the first byte of the payload (the ImageMagick raw-profile format begins with a leading byte before the name line). `eot` points one past the last byte. The `if (sp >= eot)` check ensures there is at least one byte after the skipped first byte before any dereference — so a 1-byte payload returns empty here rather than reading out of bounds.

Scans forward to the first newline that terminates the profile-name line. Each iteration dereferences *sp, advances, and checks sp against eot, so the scan cannot walk past the buffer: when sp reaches eot the function returns empty. After the newline is found, `sp++` steps over it, and another eot check ensures there is still at least one byte to read. The dereference at the top of the loop is always of a byte strictly before eot because the previous iteration's bounds check (or the line-24 check on entry) guarantees sp < eot.

Skips leading NUL, space and newline padding that separates the name line from the ASCII decimal length. Same bounded pattern: advance, then compare against eot and return empty if exhausted. On exit, sp points at the first non-whitespace, non-NUL byte, which is expected to be the first digit of the length.

Records startOfLength at the first digit, then advances sp over the entire run of ASCII digits, again bounded by an eot check each step. Because the loop only stops when it finds a non-digit that is still strictly before eot (or returns empty), it guarantees a non-digit sentinel exists in-bounds after the digit run. `sp++` then steps over the assumed trailing newline, followed by another eot check. Note the sp++ is unconditional and assumes the byte after the digits is a single separator; if it is not a newline it simply consumes one arbitrary byte.

Converts the digit run at startOfLength to a long via atol. atol reads until the first non-digit; since the digit-scan loop above already proved a non-digit exists strictly before eot, atol stops in-bounds. Two enforce() guards follow: length must be non-negative (rejects atol negative-overflow / a leading minus), and length must be no larger than half of the remaining bytes (eot - sp)/2. The second guard is the crucial memory-safety bound: it caps the declared output length to the physical number of hex characters that could remain (2 hex chars per output byte), which is what prevents the decode loop from reading past eot when the data is well-formed.

Allocates the output buffer of `length` bytes. A length of 0 only emits a debug message and still calls alloc(0). After allocation the code confirms info.size_ == length; if the allocation did not yield the requested size (e.g. allocation failure), it returns an empty DataBuf rather than proceeding. The magnitude passed to alloc is attacker-influenced (derived from the ASCII length), bounded above only by (eot - sp)/2 — i.e. by the input size.

Sets up the decode. `dp` is the write cursor into the freshly allocated output. `nibbles` is the number of hex characters to consume — two per output byte — computed as length * 2. The multiplication is done in `long` (length is long) and the result is stored into an `unsigned int`; nibbles is always even by construction, which is what keeps the paired even/odd nibble writes balanced.

The hex-decode loop, run once per nibble. At the top of each iteration enforce(sp < eot) guarantees the cursor is in-bounds. The inner while skips any byte that is not a lowercase hex digit ('0'-'9' or 'a'-'f'): it returns empty on an embedded NUL, and after each skip re-checks enforce(sp < eot), so the skip cannot walk off the end. Once a hex byte is found, the byte value is used as an index into unhex[]: for even i the high nibble is stored (16 * unhex[*sp], dp not advanced); for odd i the low nibble is added and dp is advanced. Because dp is advanced exactly once per pair (on odd i) and nibbles == 2*length, dp advances exactly `length` times, writing bytes only into indices 0..length-1 of the length-sized allocation. The three enforce(sp < eot) guards plus the (eot-sp)/2 length cap are what keep the reads inside the buffer.

Returns the decoded profile buffer. On every early-return path an empty DataBuf() is returned instead, and on any enforce() failure an Exiv2 exception is thrown; only a fully consumed, well-formed hex blob reaches this successful return.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Uncontrolled allocation sized by attacker data (CWE-770). `length` is parsed by atol() from an ASCII decimal field the attacker writes into the PNG raw-profile chunk. The only cap is enforce(length <= (eot - sp)/2), i.e. it is bounded by the input size, not by any absolute limit. A crafted (possibly zlib-inflated zTXt) chunk can therefore force a large heap allocation on demand, and a stream of such chunks/images amplifies memory pressure. There is no throttling or maximum-profile-size policy in this function, so the invariant "decoded profile size is bounded by a sane fixed maximum" is not upheld; the caller/decoder must impose a chunk-size limit to prevent memory-exhaustion denial of service.

The iTXt branch allocates and copies text.size_ bytes with no upper bound (CWE-770). An iTXt chunk (or its inflated content) can be very large, and this path performs a full-size allocation plus a full-size memcpy driven entirely by the attacker-chosen size. The copy is internally consistent so it is not a buffer overflow, but the allocation magnitude is unbounded relative to any policy, reinforcing the memory-exhaustion DoS surface. Invariant: an absolute cap on profile size must be enforced before this allocation.

The entire memory-safety of the unhex[] indexing at lines 109/111 hinges on this exact guard expression combined with the array being sized to exactly 103 ('f'+1). The loop only falls through when *sp is in ['0'..'9'] or ['a'..'f'], so unhex[*sp] indexes 48..57 or 97..102 — the last of which (102) is the final valid element with zero slack. This is currently safe, but it is a fragile, latent out-of-bounds-read contract (CWE-125): any change that widens the accepted set (e.g. accepting uppercase 'A'-'F' at 65-70, or a byte > 'f') or that lets a value above 102 reach the index would read past unhex[]. On platforms where char is signed, a high byte (>=0x80) is negative; it is caught here by *sp < '0' and skipped, so it does not reach the index — but only because of this guard. The implementation must keep the accepted-character set and the table size in lockstep. This is the same decode loop historically responsible for the readRawProfile heap over-read; the enforce(sp < eot) guards on lines 93/105 are the mitigation.

Integer truncation / narrowing (CWE-190/197). `length` is a long bounded by (eot - sp)/2, and length*2 is computed in long, but the result is stored into a 32-bit unsigned int. On a 64-bit platform with an input buffer larger than ~4 GiB (feasible for an in-memory decompressed zTXt payload), length can exceed UINT_MAX/2 and nibbles wraps to a small value. The loop then runs (long)nibbles iterations — fewer than 2*length — so it under-writes the length-sized allocation rather than overflowing it. Impact is limited (a partially-initialized profile buffer, not a memory-safety violation), but the size types are inconsistent: nibbles should be the same width as length. Invariant: the nibble counter must not narrow the length.

atol is called on startOfLength, which points into a buffer that is NOT guaranteed to be NUL-terminated. In this specific control flow it is safe because the preceding digit-scan (lines 53-60) already proved a non-digit byte exists strictly before eot, so atol stops in-bounds; and atol has no way to report overflow, which is why the two enforce() guards on lines 67-68 are needed to reject a value that overflowed to negative or to something larger than the buffer. The residual risk is contract fragility: if the digit-scan or the guards were ever weakened, atol would over-read (CWE-125) and/or an unchecked huge/negative length would flow into alloc. Invariant: a valid in-bounds non-digit terminator must always precede atol, and the >=0 and <= (eot-sp)/2 guards must both remain.

what_could_go_wrong.code: const char *sp  = (char*) text.pData_+1;          // current byte (space pointer)
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The cursor is initialized to pData_+1, unconditionally skipping the first byte on the assumption that the raw-profile format has a fixed leading byte. This is safe only because line 11 guaranteed size_ >= 1 and line 24 (if (sp >= eot)) rejects the case where the +1 lands at or past eot. It is worth flagging as the one place where pointer arithmetic assumes a minimum layout; if either guard were removed, pData_+1 on a 1-byte buffer would produce sp == eot and the first dereference (line 29) would read one byte out of bounds (CWE-125). Currently mitigated.

summary: This is a hardened version of Exiv2's PngChunk::readRawProfile hex-decoder: the three enforce(sp < eot) guards and the enforce(length <= (eot - sp)/2) cap keep the pointer walks and the unhex[] indexing in-bounds, mitigating the classic readRawProfile heap over-read family (CVE-2018-16336). The remaining risks are (1) uncontrolled, input-sized allocations in both the iTXt copy path and the raw-profile path (CWE-770 memory-exhaustion DoS, matching the sample's tag), (2) a fragile in-bounds contract where unhex[] safety depends entirely on the exact character-class guard and a table sized to 'f'+1, and (3) a long->unsigned int narrowing of the nibble count that under-writes on multi-GiB inputs.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry point; text and iTXt are wholly attacker-controlled and define the trust boundary.
Fixed-size stack buffer indexed by attacker-derived byte values at lines 109/111; size 103 == 'f'+1 with zero slack, so its safety is a load-bearing contract.
Allocation size derived from attacker ASCII length; CWE-770 unbounded allocation.
Allocation and copy sized directly by attacker-controlled text.size_; CWE-770.
long->unsigned int narrowing; can wrap on >4GiB inputs causing under-write.
atol over a not-necessarily-terminated buffer plus no built-in overflow signal; mitigated here by prior digit scan and the two enforce guards, but a fragile contract.
Unconditional +1 offset assumes a minimum layout; mitigated by size>=1 and the sp>=eot check but noted as a bounds contract.
One-past-end sentinel; correctly used only as an upper bound in comparisons, never dereferenced.
Attacker-controlled size that drives the iTXt allocation/copy; covered by the CWE-770 iTXt finding.
Guard, not a defect: rejects negative/overflowed length before allocation.
Guard: bounds the output length to the physically available hex characters; this is the fix that prevents the decode over-read, not a vulnerability.
Guards that bound every read in the decode loop; their presence is what mitigates the historical CVE-2018-16336 over-read.
Character-class filter whose exact bounds are the sole guarantee that unhex[] is not indexed out of range; latent CWE-125 contract.
Guard that makes the pData_+1 offset safe on tiny inputs.
Guard: aborts on allocation failure/short allocation before any write.
dp advances exactly length times (once per even/odd pair over nibbles==2*length), so writes stay within the length-sized allocation; not an overflow.
Exiv2 error-reporting plumbing; carries no attacker-controlled data.

findings.title: Uncontrolled memory allocation from attacker-supplied profile length
findings.bug_class: Allocation of resources without limits or throttling (memory-exhaustion DoS)
findings.invariant: The decoded-profile size must be capped by an absolute maximum (a policy limit), not merely by the size of the untrusted input, before any allocation.
findings.failure_mode: The attacker writes a large ASCII decimal into the raw-profile length field of a PNG (t/z)EXt chunk. atol yields a large `length`, bounded only by enforce(length <= (eot - sp)/2) — i.e. by the chunk size, which for a zlib-inflated zTXt chunk can be very large. info.alloc(length) then allocates that much heap on demand. A single crafted image, or many chunks/images, forces large repeated allocations and can exhaust memory, causing denial of service in any application decoding untrusted PNGs.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether DataBuf::alloc or a caller enforces an absolute maximum profile size; confirm there is a ceiling independent of the input length before this allocation.

findings.title: Unbounded iTXt profile allocation and copy
findings.bug_class: Allocation of resources without limits or throttling
findings.invariant: The iTXt copy must reject or cap payloads exceeding an absolute size limit before allocating.
findings.failure_mode: For iTXt, the function allocates and memcpy's text.size_ bytes with no upper bound. The copy itself is size-consistent (no overflow), but the allocation magnitude is fully attacker-chosen, so an oversized iTXt payload (or its inflated form) drives a large heap allocation, contributing to memory-exhaustion DoS. This is a distinct code path from the raw-profile allocation and lacks even the (eot - sp)/2 relationship.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether callers gate iTXt chunk size before invoking readRawProfile; confirm a maximum accepted chunk length exists upstream.

findings.title: unhex[] out-of-bounds read is guarded only by an exact character-class filter
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read (fixed table indexed by attacker byte)
findings.invariant: The index into unhex[] must always lie in 0..102; the set of characters allowed to reach unhex[*sp] must stay in lockstep with the table's size (103 == 'f'+1), and the enforce(sp < eot) guards must remain.
findings.failure_mode: The loop falls through only for bytes in ['0'..'9'] or ['a'..'f'], so unhex[*sp] currently indexes 48..57/97..102 — with 'f' (102) exactly the last valid slot. This is safe as written, but the table has zero slack above 'f' and the safety is entirely a property of this guard expression plus the enforce(sp < eot) bounds. If the accepted set were widened (e.g. to accept uppercase 'A'-'F', or a byte > 'f'), or an enforce guard removed, an attacker byte would index past unhex[] (stack over-read) — the classic readRawProfile decode hazard. Latent/defense-in-depth finding.
findings.what_to_confirm: That unhex[] size and the character-class guard remain consistent, and that all three enforce(sp < eot) guards (lines 93, 105) are present; confirm no code path reaches the index with *sp outside ['0'..'9','a'..'f'].

findings.title: long-to-unsigned-int narrowing of nibble count
findings.invariant: The nibble counter must have the same width as `length` so that 2*length is representable without truncation.
findings.failure_mode: length is a long bounded by (eot - sp)/2. On a 64-bit platform an in-memory (decompressed) payload larger than ~4 GiB lets length*2 exceed UINT_MAX; storing it into the 32-bit `nibbles` truncates the loop bound, so fewer than 2*length nibbles are decoded and the length-sized output buffer is left partially uninitialized. Impact is limited to a partially-filled profile (no overflow), but it is a latent inconsistency between the allocation size (long) and the decode count (unsigned int).
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether inputs of that size are reachable (max chunk/inflate size) and whether nibbles should be `long`/`size_t` to match length.

audit_checklist: Confirm an absolute maximum profile/chunk size is enforced upstream (before info.alloc(length) at line 77 and info.alloc(text.size_) at line 16) so allocation is not bounded only by input size — CWE-770.

Verify all three enforce(sp < eot) guards (lines 93, 105) and enforce(length <= (eot - sp)/2) (line 68) are present and unmodified; they are the load-bearing mitigation for the historical readRawProfile heap over-read (CVE-2018-16336).
Confirm unhex[]'s size (103) stays consistent with the character-class filter on line 94, and that no path (including uppercase hex or bytes > 'f') indexes unhex[*sp] outside 0..102 — CWE-125.
Check char signedness handling: on signed-char platforms a high byte is negative and skipped by *sp < '0'; ensure no compiler/flag change lets a negative byte reach unhex[(int)*sp].
Change `unsigned int nibbles` to a width matching `length` to avoid truncation on multi-GiB inputs (line 89) — CWE-197.
Verify DataBuf::alloc's failure/zero-size semantics match the info.size_ != length check (line 78), including alloc(0) behavior.
Confirm the unconditional pData_+1 (line 21) and the two step-over sp++ (lines 37, 61) can never advance past eot given the size>=1 and sp>=eot / sp==eot guards.
